observations.recommendation:
Based on the identified
deficiencies and to address
the recurring issues, we
recommended and the
Local Chief Executive
agreed to:
a. Require the General
Services Office (GSO) to
(i) reconcile the results of the physical inventory
count with the records of the Accounting Unit to
ensure consistency and accuracy in Property,
Plant, and Equipment (PPE) records; and promptly
prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report of
Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) in accordance
with Section 168 of COA Circular No. 92-386 to
facilitate the identification and disposal of
unserviceable PPE.
b. Instruct the Disposal Committee to initiate
disposal procedures for unserviceable PPE to
mitigate the risk of asset mismanagement and
financial discrepancies, as outlined in Section 162 of
the same circular.
c. Task the Inventory Committee in prioritizing the
one-time cleansing of PPE account balances as
per COA Circular No. 2020-006 to address
historical reliability issues in financial statements.
Allocate dedicated time and resources, including
relieving the inventory committee of regular duties,
to conduct thorough inventory verification and
reconciliation activities.
d. Allocate additional resources to the Accounting
Unit and General Services Office to address the
staffing shortage and ensure that sufficient
personnel are available to maintain ledger cards,
conduct physical inventory counts, and facilitate
disposal procedures for unserviceable PPE.

We recommended and the
Local Chief Executive
Agreed to:
(a) instruct the Local
Development Council
(LDC) to
i. identify stakeholders
by key planning and
development sectors or
coverage;
ii. prepare an action plan
for approaching and
involving each person or
group;
iii. develop and
implement an Information
and Education Campaign
(IEC) plan to inform and
solicit support and
commitment of the
identified stakeholders and
the general public;
iv. involve the general
community by conducting
focus group discussions,
interviews, surveys, and
other innovative forms of
indirect participation in
case there are still not
enough information to
create a comprehensive
plans and programs; and
(b) require the
Punong Barangays within
the Municipality to submit
their respective Barangay
Development Plan (BDP)
to be incorporated in the
preparation of updated
CLUP and CDP.

We recommended and the
Local Chief Executive
agreed to instruct the Local
Disaster Risk Reduction
Management Council to:
a. prepare the request for
an appropriation ordinance
for the utilization of
unexpended LDRRM Fund
once the resolution for the
adoption of the amended
LDRRMFIP was approved;
b. conduct adequate
research and planning prior
to the preparation of
LDRRMFIP;
c. use Program of Works
and project proposals with
estimate of expenses as
basis for the proposed
budget of each PPA to be
included in the
LDRRMFIP;
d. set a realistic and
specific schedule of
implementation for each
PPA to ensure the timely
enjoyment of benefits
therefrom by the
community; and
e. submit the budget
proposals in accordance
with the schedule for
budget preparation to have
an ample time for review
and revision.

We recommended and the
Local Chief Executive
agreed to:
a. instruct the Market
Committee:
(i) to evaluate the
provision in the Market
Code limiting stallholders
to lease a maximum of two
stalls. They should assess
its relevance in the current
market context. If deemed
still pertinent, efforts
should be made to ensure
strict adherence to this
provision. Otherwise,
update the specific
provision in the Market
Code;
(ii) re-assess the space
occupied by each stall and
categorize them accurately
to ensure a more precise
computation of rental fees;
b. instruct the Municipal
Treasurer to
(i) strictly implement the
current rental rates as
specified in the Market
Code;
(ii) follow the mode and
frequency of collection of
market rentals stated in
Section 1, Chapter III of the
Market Code, where
issuance of Official Receipt
is employed;
c. conduct investigation
and promptly revoke the
rent of the stallholders who
are found to be subleasing
their stalls to third parties;
and
d. immediately revoke
the rental agreement for the
abandoned stalls and
implement procedures for
vacated stalls.

We recommended and the
LCE agreed to instruct the
GFP to:
(a) create a multi-year GAD
Agenda as basis for the
annual GAD Plans and
Budget by using the
templates provided in PCW
Memorandum Circular No.
2018-04 dated September
19, 2018; and
(c) timely submit to the
Audit Team the GAD
Accomplishment Report in
compliance with COA
Circular No. 2014-001.

The COA recommended to
a. Work with the
Municipal Budget Office to
reassess allocations and
potentially reallocate funds
to support the recruitment
of additional personnel
dedicated to SWM and
accommodate additional
funds for fuel and tipping
fee allocation, as well as
upgrading equipment;
b. Instruct the MENRO
to (i) establish volunteer
programs within local
communities to support
waste segregation,
collection, and awareness
campaigns, relieving some
of the operational burdens;
(ii) explore additional
innovative revenue-
generating opportunities
within the SWM project;
c. Diversify funding
sources by (i) exploring
foreign assistance
programs, (ii) seeking
grants from concerned
agencies to mitigate the
financial shortfall and
enhance the sustainability
of waste management
efforts; (iii) developing a
comprehensive public
awareness campaign to
highlight the importance of
environmental fees and
communicate their direct
impact on sustainable waste
management within the
Municipality; and (iv)
strengthening enforcement
efforts and ensure strict
compliance with SWM
regulations, generating
meaningful revenues from
penalties and fines to
support SWM initiatives.
d. Ensure that the
remuneration for waste
management staff aligns
with labor standards.
e. Provide comprehensive
training and educational
programs to barangay
officials and residents,
emphasizing waste
segregation,
composting, recycling, and
sustainable livelihood
opportunities from
recyclable waste. This can
be facilitated through
partnerships with
environmental
organizations, academic
institutions, or government
agencies; and
f. Foster collaboration
with the Liga Ng Mga
Barangay to (i) organize a
core group of Area/Street
Coordinators to support the
Barangay Ecological Solid
Waste Management
Committee (BESWMC) in
executing their programs
and activities effectively.;
and (ii) Facilitate the
identification of appropriate
open spaces for the
establishment of Material
Recovery Facilities (MRFs)
in each barangay or clusters
of barangays, as well as
eco-centers.

We recommended and
Management agreed to:
a. establish clear
timelines for each step in
the transaction process,
enabling staff to adhere to
predefined benchmarks and
promoting a sense of
urgency in task completion.
Standardized timelines will
facilitate consistent
monitoring, identification
of inefficiencies, and timely
intervention to address
delays;
b. implement a routing
slip based on the
established timelines to
ensure a consistent and
standardized process for the
movement of documents;
and
c. review and adjust staff
levels to match the
workload, aiming to reduce
delays caused by
insufficient manpower.

Based on the identified deficiencies and to address the recurring issues,
we recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
(a) Require the GSO to (i) reconcile the results of the physical inventory
count with the records of the Accounting Unit to ensure consistency and
accuracy in PPE records; and promptly prepare the IIRUP in
accordance with Section 168 of COA Circular No. 92-386 to facilitate the
identification and disposal of unserviceable PPE.
(b) Instruct the Disposal Committee to initiate disposal procedures for
unserviceable PPE to mitigate the risk of asset mismanagement and
financial discrepancies, as outlined in Section 162 of the same Circular.
(c) Task the Inventory Committee in prioritizing the one-time cleansing of
PPE account balances as per COA Circular No. 2020-006 to address
historical reliability issues in financial statements. Allocate dedicated
time and resources, including relieving the inventory committee of
regular duties, to conduct thorough inventory verification and
reconciliation activities.
(d) Ensure that all insurable properties are adequately insured to protect
government assets in the event of loss or damage to properties due to
fire, earthquake, storm or other casualty.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the:
(a) MHRMO to furnish the Municipal Accountant with the computed
total money value of leave credits of all regular employees that will be
the basis of the latter in recording in the books of accounts; and
(b) Municipal Accountant to recognize in the books the total money value
of leave credits computed by the MHRMO by debiting Terminal Leave
Benefits and crediting Leave Benefits Payable, in accordance with
IPSAS 39 and COA Circular No. 2024-009.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure the
effective and efficient implementation of development projects to fully
maximize the provision of benefits to the intended beneficiaries.
Furthermore, the Municipality is encouraged to continue prioritizing and
investing in high-impact capital expenditure projects in the utilization of
its 20% Development Fund.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
a. assess the necessity of engaging consultants, ensuring that their roles are
essential and that outputs are clearly defined and measurable in their
contracts to substantiate that their services provide distinct values that
cannot be fulfilled by the existing municipal officials/department heads;
b. in cases where consultants are deemed necessary, (i) engage only experts
in their respective fields, (ii) adhere to the correct procurement process in
accordance with regulations and guidelines to ensure transparency,
accountability, and competitiveness, and (iii) ensure that all payments for
consultancy services are supported with complete documentation to verify
compliance with procurement laws and proper use of funds; and
c. require the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the payments to consultants
in accordance with the Local Government Chart of Accounts and update
financial reports to ensure the accurate classification of expenses.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to (a) continue
its efforts to substantially comply with the guidelines on the
appropriation, utilization, and accounting of LDRRMF following the rules
and regulations under RA No. 10121 or the Philippine Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Act of 2010 and COA Circular No. 2012-002
dated September 12, 2012; and (b) ensure the full implementation of the
pre-disaster preparedness programs for increased awareness and
knowledge in disaster management.

We recommended and Management agreed to (a) continue its efforts to
substantially comply with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum
Circular No. 2013-01 on the appropriation, utilization, and reporting of
GAD; and (b) ensure the timely submission to the Audit Team of the
GAD Plan and Budget and Accomplishment Report in compliance with
COA Circular No. 2014-001.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the
LSB to (a) furnish the concerned officials with a copy of the SEF Budget
within the prescribed timelines; (b) furnish the Department of Education
(DepEd) Central Office, the local Sanggunian concerned and the Regional
Offices of the DBM and DILG with copies of SEF Budget Accountability
Form No. 1; and (c) post the utilization reports of the SEF Budget for all
quarters of the year in the Municipal website, and/or in at least three
conspicuous public places within the indicated timelines, to fully comply
with the requirements of Item 6.1 of the DEPED-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s.
2017.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to (a) initiate
proper coordination with the authorized lending institution - (Philippine
Veterans Bank) for the amendment of the existing MOA to pay a
reasonable service fee to the Municipality for every remittance of
employees’ loans; and (b) stop serving as a collecting agent of employees’
loans on behalf of the unaccredited lending institution.

We commended and Management agreed for the prompt remittance of
the taxes withheld during the current year to the BIR. This
notwithstanding, we recommended the LCE to continually ensure the
proper withholding and timely remittance of taxes in conformity with
Revenue Memorandum Circular Nos. 23-007 and 23-2012, and BIR
Revenue Regulation No. 2-98.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the Municipal
Accountant to continue the proper deduction and timely remittance of
GSIS contributions in accordance with the provisions of R.A. No. 8291, to
guarantee that all personnel are covered by the benefits under the law.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the
Accountant to ensure the prompt and full remittance of all the
contributions to PhilHealth, in accordance with National Health
Insurance Act of 2013 and PhilHealth Circular No. 1, s. 2014.

We recommended and Management agreed to continue complying with
the provisions of R.A. No. 9679 on the proper withholding and timely
remittance of Pag-IBIG contributions and loan amortizations.

We recommended and
Management agreed to
ensure that:
(a) the officials concerned
adhere with the timely
disclosure of project
updates and financial
information to relevant
ess oversight bodies and the
public to enhance
transparency and
accountability; and
(b) all parties involved in
the contract, including the
Municipality and the
contractor, adhere strictly to
the terms outlined in the
contract. This includes
clarifying responsibilities
and liabilities regarding
project delays and damages
incurred during
construction.

In view of the above, we
recommended and the
Local Chief Executive
agreed to:
a. Instruct the BPLO to:
(i) ensure accurate
assessment of business
taxes; and (ii) take
necessary measures to
collect any outstanding
business tax owed by
businesses operating in the
area; and
b. Continue imposing
and diligently collecting
business taxes from all
eligible entities.

We recommended and Management agreed to
continue to comply with the
submission of the required
PRDP reports on time for
future PRDP projects to
ensure the timely reporting
and liquidation of the funds
to the source or sponsoring
agency.

We recommended and Management agreed to
consistently seek funding
channels akin to PRDP for
the implementation of
future similar projects that
would benefit the public
and significantly contribute
to the overall enhancement
of rural livelihoods.

We commended Management for the prompt
remittance of the taxes
withheld during the current
year to the BIR. This
notwithstanding, we
recommended the LCE to
continually ensure the
proper withholding and
timely remittance of taxes
in conformity with Revenue
Memorandum Circular Nos.
23-007 and 23-2012, and
BIR Revenue Regulation
No. 2-98

We recommended and the
LCE agreed to direct the
Municipal Accountant to
continue the monthly
deduction and remittance of
GSIS contributions in
accordance with R.A. No.
8291.

We commended Management for the faithful
compliance with the
withholding and prompt
remittance of premiums to
the PhilHealth and
recommended the
continued observance
thereof for the benefits of
its employees.

We commended
Management in complying
with the provisions of R.A.
No. 9679 on the proper
withholding and prompt
remittance of contributions
and loan amortizations, and
advised the continued
observance of the
requirements of the Act.

observations.recommendation:
Based on the identified deficiencies and to address the recurring issues, we recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
Require the General Services Office (GSO) to (i) reconcile the results of the physical inventory count with the records of the Accounting Unit to ensure consistency and accuracy in Property, Plant, and Equipment (PPE) records; and promptly prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) in accordance with Section 168 of COA Circular No. 92-386 to facilitate the identification and disposal of unserviceable PPE.
Instruct the Disposal Committee to initiate disposal procedures for unserviceable PPE to mitigate the risk of asset mismanagement and financial discrepancies, as outlined in Section 162 of the same circular.
Task the Inventory Committee in prioritizing the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances as per COA Circular No. 2020-006 to address historical reliability issues in financial statements. Allocate dedicated time and resources, including relieving the inventory committee of regular duties, to conduct thorough inventory verification and reconciliation activities.
Allocate additional resources to the Accounting Unit and General Services Office to address the staffing shortage and ensure that sufficient personnel are available to maintain ledger cards, conduct physical inventory counts, and facilitate disposal procedures for unserviceable PPE.

We recommended and Management agreed to ensure that:
(a) the officials concerned adhere with the timely disclosure of project updates and financial information to relevant oversight bodies and the public to enhance transparency and accountability; and
(e) all parties involved in the contract, including the Municipality and the contractor, adhere strictly to the terms outlined in the contract. This includes clarifying responsibilities and liabilities regarding project delays and damages incurred during construction.

In view of the above, we recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
Instruct the BPLO to: (i) ensure accurate assessment of business taxes; and (ii) take necessary measures to collect any outstanding business tax owed by businesses operating in the area; and
Continue imposing and diligently collecting business taxes from all eligible entities.

We recommended and Management agreed to continue to comply with the submission of the required PRDP reports on time for future PRDP projects to ensure the timely reporting and liquidation of the funds to the source or sponsoring agency.

We recommended and Management agreed to consistently seek funding channels akin to PRDP for the implementation of future similar projects that would benefit the public and significantly contribute to the overall enhancement of rural livelihoods.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the GFP to:
(a) create a multi-year GAD Agenda as basis for the annual GAD Plans and Budget by using the templates provided in PCW Memorandum Circular No. 2018-04 dated September 19, 2018; and
(b) timely submit to the Audit Team the GAD Accomplishment Report in compliance with COA Circular No. 2014-001.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Work with the Municipal Budget Office to reassess allocations and potentially reallocate funds to support the recruitment of additional personnel dedicated to SWM and accommodate additional funds for fuel and tipping fee allocation, as well as upgrading equipment;
Instruct the MENRO to (i) establish volunteer programs within local communities to support waste segregation, collection, and awareness campaigns, relieving some of the operational burdens; and (ii) explore additional innovative revenue-generating opportunities within the SWM project;
Diversify funding sources by (i) exploring foreign assistance programs, (ii) seeking grants from concerned agencies to mitigate the financial shortfall and enhance the sustainability of waste management efforts; (iii) developing a comprehensive public awareness campaign to highlight the importance of environmental fees and communicate their direct impact on sustainable waste management within the Municipality; and (iv) strengthening enforcement efforts and ensure strict compliance with SWM regulations, generating meaningful revenues from penalties and fines to support SWM initiatives.
Ensure that the remuneration for waste management staff aligns with labor standards.
Provide comprehensive training and educational programs to barangay officials and residents, emphasizing waste segregation, composting, recycling, and sustainable livelihood opportunities from recyclable waste. This can be facilitated through partnerships with environmental organizations, academic institutions, or government agencies; and
Foster collaboration with the Liga Ng Mga Barangay to (i) organize a core group of Area/Street Coordinators to support the Barangay Ecological Solid Waste Management Committee (BESWMC) in executing their programs and activities effectively.; and (ii) Facilitate the identification of appropriate open spaces for the establishment of Material Recovery Facilities (MRFs) in each barangay or clusters of barangays, as well as eco-centers.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
establish clear timelines for each step in the transaction process, enabling staff to adhere to predefined benchmarks and promoting a sense of urgency in task completion. Standardized timelines will facilitate consistent monitoring, identification of inefficiencies, and timely intervention to address delays;
implement a routing slip based on the established timelines to ensure a consistent and standardized process for the movement of documents; and
review and adjust staff levels to match the workload, aiming to reduce delays caused by insufficient manpower.

We commended Management for the prompt remittance of the taxes withheld during the current year to the BIR. This notwithstanding, we recommended the LCE to continually ensure the proper withholding and timely remittance of taxes in conformity with Revenue Memorandum Circular Nos. 23-007 and 23-2012, and BIR Revenue Regulation No. 2-98.
We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to continue the monthly deduction and remittance of GSIS contributions in accordance with R.A. No. 8291.

We commended Management for the faithful compliance with the withholding and prompt remittance of premiums to the PhilHealth and recommended the continued observance thereof for the benefits of its employees.

We commended Management in complying with the provisions of R.A. No. 9679 on the proper withholding and prompt remittance of contributions and loan amortizations, and advised the continued observance of the requirements of the Act.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their records of Real Property Tax (RPT) and SEF receivables in order to report reliably the said account in the financial statements. No. 10154 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR).

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the concerned employees/disbursing officers to observe the pertinent provisions of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, particularly on the proper preparation of liquidation reports/report of disbursement for cash advances.

Due to the foregoing, we reiterated our prior years’ recommendation and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the concerned officials to exert all effort to collect the long outstanding receivables from the loan beneficiaries to ensure the continuity of the livelihood projects and to give other prospective beneficiaries equal opportunity in availing capital for the livelihood program.  We also recommended and Management agreed to comply with all the requirements and consider filing request for write-off for all established dormant accounts as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 so that the balances of loans receivable may be reported reliably in the financial statements.

We recommended and Management agreed to comply with all the requirements and procedures in the filing of request for write-off for all dormant accounts as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 in order to reliably report the balances of Due from NGAs and Due from LGUs in the financial statements.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to refund to source agency or remit to Bureau of Treasury the unexpended balances of completed projects as prescribed by COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the OIC - GSO to 
(a) continue the conduct of physical inventory of all PPE of the Municipality and submit related reports to the Office of the Auditor; 
(b) make reconciliation with the records of the Accounting Unit in order to determine the noted discrepancies; and 
(c) continue to update PPE records such as property cards, Property Acknowledgement Receipts and inventory tags.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
(a) instruct Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their cashbooks with the accounting records at least quarterly as required by Section 181(c) of Government Accounting and Auditing Manual (GAAM) Volume I; and
(b) instruct Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary ledgers for 
general ledger account Cash – Local Treasury in the General Fund.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require MPDC to
 (a) refrain from including expenditure items that are not allowed to be charged against the Development Fund (DF); 
(b) optimally utilize budgeted DF; and 
(c) comply with the reportorial requirements of DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-1, dated February 22, 2017.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive and the Local School Board agreed to:

 (a) refrain from charging ineligible expenditure items from SEF; and 
(b) comply with the reportorial requirements of DepEd, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017, dated January 19, 2017.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed that the LDRRMO

 (a) consider the more urgent projects listed in the LDRRMP in the allocation and utilization of the LDRRMF; 
(b) strictly monitor the progress of the slope protection project so that its constituents can immediately benefit from it; and
(c) comply with the reportorial requirements of JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013.

We recommended and the MSWMB and the MENRO agreed to 
(a) consider updating the Solid Waste Management Plan of the Municipality in order to ensure its sustainability, viability, effectiveness and relevance as required by Section 16 of RA 9003; and 
(b) encourage barangays with no functional MRF to establish one which will receive, sort, process and store compostable and recyclable material as provided by Section 32 of RA 9003.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the 
a) BAC Chairman and BAC Secretariat to submit the lacking documentary requirements to the Office of the Auditor and to ensure compliance with the pertinent provisions of 2016 Revised IRR; and 
(b) Municipal Accountant to ensure that disbursement vouchers are supported with complete documentary requirements.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Municipal Treasurer to 
(a) update and post notice of delinquency for property taxes at the main entrance of the municipal building and in publicly accessible and conspicuous place in each barangay where these properties located; 
(b) seek assistance from the concerned barangays for the issuance of Notice of Delinquency and to conduct an information dissemination regarding the importance of paying taxes promptly and the consequences of non-payment of taxes; if warranted, enforced the provision of Sections 258 and 260 of the RA 7160 in the collection of delinquent taxes; and
(c) impose the 25% surcharge on rentals not paid on time, revocation of lease contract and the stall be declared vacant and advertise for lease in accordance with the Municipal Ordinance No. 07-2000 and  direct the Market Supervisor to strictly monitor the implementation of the market policies to improve collections.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct HRMO V to strictly follow the CSC Rule on leave and CSC Resolution No. 992064 in the processing of leave monetization of employees of the Municipality.

We recommended and the Local Council for the Protection of Children agreed to present in its Plan and Budget and Accomplishment Report only of those programs/projects/activities appropriated out of its 1% Internal Revenue Allotment in compliance with Section 15 of RA 9344.

9.9 We recommended that the (a) Municipal Environment and Natural Resources Officer (MENRO), the Health Officer and other concerned municipal officials to immediately coordinate with the barangay officials concerned  for the strict implementation of the Municipality of Talavera Solid Waste Management Ordinance of 2007, particularly on the prohibition on the littering, dumping and open burning of solid wastes like plastic, dried leaves and other farm wastes; (b) Municipal Agriculturist encourage farmers to do composting of farm waste to enrich the soil with natural fertilizers; and (c) Municipal Environment Officer together with the Municipal Health Officer conduct proper information dissemination like conducting seminars, production of posters/tarpaulins on the harmful effects to the health of the public of smoke from burning of solid waste.

We recommended and management agreed that members of Municipal School Board:

prepare a balanced budget with conscious and concerted efforts of observing compliance with the legal and regulatory requirements on SEF budgeting, allocation and utilization;

request for inputs from the Deped official/School Division Superintendent in the prioritization of SEF projects and expenditures in accordance with the DepEd criteria and with due regard to the provisions of laws, rules and regulations prescribing the limitations on the use of the fund; and

maximize the utilization of SEF to attain its objectives and take appropriate actions such as realignment or reprograming, if necessary.

We recommended and management agreed to utilize the 20% Development Fund in accordance with DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 to contribute in the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management, for the benefits of the intended beneficiaries, and to avoid the sanctions provided under RA7160 and other applicable laws.

We recommended and management agreed that the MSWMB and the MENRO shall:

exert effort to expedite the establishment and completion of the Sanitary Landfill;
 
involve closely the Barangay officials in the strict implementation on the existing environmental laws and intensify the information campaign on the “no segregation, no collection policy and the “no-littering policy” among the residents; and 

(c) exercise strong political will to implement the environmental laws on preserving and protecting the environment as mandated under Republic Act  No. 9003.

We recommended that all offices of the municipality fully comply with the requirements of COA Circular No. 96-010 and Volume II of NGAS for LGUs by filling out all necessary data as indicated in the Purchase Order.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer stop the practice of retaining or deducting from the salaries of municipal officials and employees the repayment of their loans with non-government or private lending institutions or associations  pursuant to Section 262 of Government Accounting Manual,  Volume I.
